findings.description: Page title/header formatting is less distinct in the HTML than in the PDF.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows a centered, bold report title with clearer hierarchy and spacing, while the HTML presents the same header text with reduced visual emphasis and looser structure.

findings.description: The HTML includes fabricated site/theme code and an invented document wrapper not present in the PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML contains text/code such as "Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS", "@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Source+Sans+3...')", and CSS variables like "--nc-navy" and "--nc-blue". None of this appears anywhere in the PDF, which is only a scanned/tabular report.

findings.description: Heading structure is not faithfully represented in semantic HTML headings.
findings.evidence: The PDF has visible title/header text: "Utilities Sales Distribution", "Gas, Power, Telecommunications, and Video Programming", "Distribution Report for Qtr 10/01/2017 - 12/31/2017", and "Distribution Date March 15, 2018". In the HTML these appear only as plain paragraphs inside `<div class="page-header">` rather than as structured headings (`h1`/`h2` etc.).

findings.description: Page footer content is duplicated in a way that does not reflect the original per-page placement.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows one footer per page (e.g., "Page 1 of 11", "Page 2 of 11", etc., each on its respective page). The HTML appends eleven separate `<div class="page-footer">` blocks consecutively at the end of the document, rather than preserving them in page context.
